title: Switching Branches
intro: You're juggling two features: dark mode and a search bar. You need to switch between branches to work on each one. But be careful -- Git won't let you switch if you have unsaved changes that could be lost.

teach.title: Switching Branches = Changing Rooms
teach.body: Think of branches like different rooms in a house, each set up for a different project. When you switch branches, Git instantly rearranges your project folder to match that branch's version of the files. It's like walking into a different room where everything is set up exactly how you left it.

teach.title: Switching Branches = Changing TV Channels
teach.body: Think of switching branches like changing channels on a TV with a built-in DVR. Each channel (branch) has its own recorded content. When you switch channels, the picture changes instantly. But if you were recording something on the current channel, you need to hit "save" first -- otherwise your recording gets corrupted. That's why you commit before switching.

teach.title: Commit Before You Switch!
teach.body: This is the most important rule: **always commit (or stash) your changes before switching branches.** If you have uncommitted changes, Git might refuse to switch to protect your work. Uncommitted changes could get mixed up between branches or be lost entirely.

teach.title: The Switching Workflow
teach.code:
git status                          # Check for uncommitted changes
git add .                           # Stage changes
git commit -m "Save my progress"    # Commit first!
git checkout main                   # Now safe to switch
git checkout -b feature/search-bar  # Create + switch in one step
teach.annotation: Always check `git status` before switching. If it shows changes, commit them first.

teach.body: Being able to juggle multiple features at once is what makes you valuable on a team. Vibecoding an app with AI? You'll constantly switch between branches for different features -- this skill keeps your work organized and your clients happy.
Always commit your changes before switching branches -- use `git checkout <branch>` or `git switch <branch>` to move between them.

steps.instruction: You're on the dark-mode branch and have just finished some changes. Check the status before switching.
steps.output:
On branch feature/dark-mode

Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)

	modified:   style.css

no changes added to commit (use "git add" to track)
steps.hint: Type 'git status' to see if you have any uncommitted changes.

steps.instruction: You have uncommitted changes to style.css. Save them with a commit before switching branches.
steps.hint: Stage the file first with 'git add style.css'.

steps.output:
[feature/dark-mode 5f6a7b8] Add dark mode styles
 1 file changed, 42 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
steps.hint: Use 'git commit -m' followed by a descriptive message in quotes.

steps.instruction: Now safely switch back to the main branch.
steps.hint: Use 'git checkout main' or 'git switch main'.

steps.instruction: Create and switch to a new search feature branch in a single command.
steps.hint: The shortcut is 'git checkout -b <name>' which creates and switches in one step. Or use 'git switch -c <name>'.

exercise.completionMessage: Nice! You committed your work before switching (important!), then created a new branch with the shortcut command. Remember: always commit or stash your changes before switching branches, or Git may refuse to switch to protect your work.
